lines.text: The afternoon corridor was empty. I hurried toward class, arms full of textbooks.
At the corner, a warm "wall" blocked my path — textbooks scattered everywhere. I looked up and met Morven's deep, unfathomable eyes.
"Walking without watching where you're going?"
He said it coldly, yet bent down to pick up every single book for me.
The moment our fingertips touched, my heart skipped a beat.
Chasing a runaway notebook to the rooftop, I found Morven leaning alone against the railing.
The sunset gilded his stern profile in soft gold.
He noticed me but, for once, didn't turn away.
"...Come sit."
He patted the spot beside him. It was the first time I saw softness in his expression. The evening breeze stirred his hair, and my heart pounded in the silence.

stories.title: Sharing an Umbrella in the Rain

lines.text: A sudden downpour after school — I stood at the gate, helpless.
A black umbrella silently appeared above my head.
"Don't catch a cold."
Morven said it expressionlessly, yet the umbrella was tilted mostly over me.
The rain drummed like a heartbeat. Our shoulders were almost touching.
At the intersection, he suddenly stopped —
"...Tomorrow, I'll walk you home."
It wasn't a question. It was a statement.
On the rooftop, Morven did something rare — he asked to meet me.
"Look at that star."
He pointed into the distance. I followed his hand — but he gently turned my face toward him.
"I'm talking about the one right here."
His voice was low, and for the first time, warm.
Under the stars, he leaned in slowly, foreheads touching.
"Since that day in the corridor... you've been my only exception."
His lips, cool with a hint of mint, gently pressed against mine.
Backstage at the school festival, I was fumbling with my makeup in a panic.
The door opened — Morven stood there in his costume.
"You need help."
He picked up the lip brush, his touch unexpectedly gentle.
In the mirror, his focused expression was like an artist tracing a masterpiece.
The warmth on my lips left my hands nowhere to rest.
"Perfect."
At the festival ball, I sat alone in the corner.
A slender hand reached out —
"This dance, I only want with you."
His hand rested steadily on my waist, guiding me in a spin.
Amidst everyone's gaze, his world seemed to hold only me.
"Every dance from now on... save it for me."
He whispered in my ear, his warm breath making my face burn even hotter.
Walking in the rain together again — this time, I took the initiative and linked my arm through his.
He stiffened for a moment, then laced his fingers through mine, holding tight.
The clouds parted, and a rainbow stretched across the sky.
"You're like that rainbow — bursting into my grey world and painting it with every color."
He lowered his head and pressed a kiss to my forehead —
"Never disappear."
At graduation, Morven walked toward me in front of the entire school.
He took a lip-shaped pendant from his pocket and fastened it around my neck himself.
"This is my mark. From now on, your smile belongs only to me."
Amid the crowd's cheers, he gently tilted my chin up, and a lingering, passionate kiss fell.
In this moment, the whole world was ours.
Daniel's English class is always packed.
As he read poetry aloud, his gaze swept the room — and lingered on me for three extra seconds.
"This poem reminds me of someone."
Deep blue eyes held starlight. I looked down, cheeks burning, my classmates' whispers fading like gauze.
After school, only Daniel and I remained in the classroom.
He sat beside me grading papers, a faint cedar scent drifting from him.
"You work too hard."
His long fingers pointed at my mistake. To read that line, we leaned closer and closer.
"Did you know? Your eyes hold an entire spring."
Winter break — Daniel went home to visit family. Every day I passed his empty classroom, and my heart felt like something was missing.
A handwritten letter arrived from across the ocean —
"Chérie, every flower in Paris reminds me of the one I miss. The evening breeze along the Seine cannot compare to the softness of your smile."
The stationery carried a faint perfume — his signature cedar.
I pressed the letter to my chest. The snow outside reflected the curve of my smile.
First day of the new term — I was stamping my feet in the cold at the school gate.
A warm coat draped over my shoulders from behind. The familiar cedar scent enveloped me instantly.
"I am back."
His voice reached my ears, low and tender.
"In Paris, every step I took, I was thinking of how to get back to you faster."
He gently wiped the tears from the corners of my eyes, foreheads touching —
A kiss tasting of coffee and cedar fell amidst the swirling snow.
Daniel made a rare exception and offered a perfume appreciation class.
He took out an antique perfume bottle and walked slowly through the classroom.
"This perfume is called Heartbeat. Its top note is rose, its middle note is jasmine, and its base note..."
He paused beside me, leaning in slightly —
"...is cedar and longing."
The bottle was gently placed on my desk, my initials engraved on the glass.
On the night of the cultural festival, Daniel led me to the back garden.
Under the moonlight, he drew an exquisite perfume bottle from his jacket — the liquid inside shimmered like a galaxy in the moonlight.
"I blended this myself. There's only one bottle in the entire world."
"Its name is... A Love Letter to Her."
His gaze was more brilliant than the starlight in the bottle.
Daniel took me to a hidden garden behind the school hill.
He had planted roses and jasmine with his own hands — the very ingredients of the Heartbeat perfume.
"Every morning I'm here, wondering what you're doing, and if maybe... you think of me too sometimes."
He picked a half-opened rose and tucked it behind my ear.
The sunset bathed the garden in gold, and his gaze was so tender it could break your heart.
On graduation day, Daniel placed an antique perfume bottle in my palm.
The liquid inside refracted a rainbow in the sunlight.
"This is Eternity — there is no base note, because our story will never end."
He knelt on one knee and retrieved a ring from a hidden compartment in the bottle's base —
"Will you let me be your forever?"
His blue eyes glistened with tears, yet his smile outshone the sun.
In his embrace, I caught the scent of a future that belonged to just the two of us.
Final exam results were out, and I was summoned to the principal's office.
Pushing open the door, I found Vincent reviewing documents, his gaze sharp as a hawk behind silver-rimmed glasses.
"Your grades have slipped. I won't allow my students to slack off."
I met his gaze defiantly. A flicker of surprise crossed his eyes.
"You have spirit. Then prove it to me with results."
For a week straight, I found a warm cup of tea and a handwritten note beside my desk —
"Keep going today too."
The handwriting was bold and forceful — Vincent's.
One late night of overtime, he appeared at the door —
"Still not leaving? I'll walk you home."
His tone brooked no argument, yet as I stepped out, he quietly draped his coat over my shoulders.
On a stormy night, I found Vincent alone in his office, sorting through old photographs.
One of them showed him in his youth, standing beside my father.
"Your father... was the person I admired most. He asked me to look after you."
He rarely showed such vulnerability. Thunder crashed outside as he removed his glasses, letting his guard down for the first time.
I stepped closer and placed my hand over his.
Morning light filtered through the blinds of the principal's office.
Vincent stood at the window, his back to me.
I told myself this was just a promise...
"But seeing you stay up late for me, worry about me, care for me... I can't lie to myself anymore."
He turned around, and the gaze behind his glasses was impossibly tender.
"Can you feel it? How fast it beats for you."
He walked toward me and placed my hand over his heart.
After the school festival banquet, Vincent led me to the century-old ginkgo tree deep within the campus.
"This tree has witnessed countless youths, but I've never brought anyone else here."
Moonlight filtered through the leaves.
"Tonight, it belongs only to us."
His palm was warm and dry. When he gripped my hand, it felt like he was holding the entire world.
On a weekend, Vincent and I were drawn to the same ancient book in the library.
He stood behind me turning pages, one arm naturally circling around me to hold the spine.
"This line of Latin means..."
His voice resonated beside my ear, low and rich as a cello.
My heart was racing so fast he must have noticed — because the faintest curve appeared at the corner of his lips.
At the school festival ball, Vincent wore a black suit and had taken off his usual glasses.
"Tonight, I'm just an ordinary man."
He extended his hand. On the dance floor, his gaze was so intent I couldn't meet it.
"You know, running a school is nothing compared to governing my own heart."
"And you... are the variable I can never control."
At graduation, Vincent personally adjusted my tassel.
"You've graduated. It's time I honored my promise."
Before everyone, he produced a book-shaped pendant — inside was engraved the date of our first confrontation.
"From that day on, I knew every decision for the rest of my life would include you."
The cafeteria was packed at lunch hour. I wandered with my tray, unable to find a seat.
"There's room here."
A warm voice called out. Leo smiled as he moved his bag aside, sunlight streaming through the window onto his clean profile.
"Do you eat here often? How come I've never seen you before?"
He handed me a napkin. His fingers were long and fair, and my heart skipped for no reason.
In home economics class, Leo and I were paired up to bake a cake.
He looked surprisingly good in an apron.
"I'll whisk the egg whites, you handle the mixing."
He divided the tasks naturally, occasionally leaning in to check my progress, our shoulders gently brushing.
"You've got flour on the corner of your mouth."
He smiled and reached to wipe it away. His fingertips were warm, and I forgot to breathe.
Studying for finals until late, my stomach ached from hunger.
I tiptoed into the kitchen, only to find Leo already cooking noodles.
"Can't sleep either?"
He smiled and grabbed an extra bowl.
"My grandma says late-night noodles need an egg to warm the stomach."
He pushed the steaming bowl toward me. In the dim light, his smile made my heart flutter more than any gourmet meal ever could.
At the school festival, Leo waited at the baking booth all afternoon just for me.
"I made this — try it."
He handed me an exquisite chocolate. I bit into it and found a heart-shaped candy inside, along with a note —
"Every dessert is something I want to say to you, and you are the sweetest one of all."
In the sunset, he gently took my hand —
"Let me be your personal chef for the rest of your life, okay?"
Leo mysteriously led me to a Michelin-starred restaurant hidden in a narrow alley.
"The chef here is my mentor. He says good food should be shared with the most special person."
By flickering candlelight, he explained each dish to me one by one, the focus in his eyes impossible to look away from.
"And you are the one I want to share everything with."
Late at night, Leo sneaked me into the school's home economics classroom.
"Tonight we're making a dish that belongs only to us."
He tied on an apron and handed me one. Under the stars, we chopped, seasoned, and stir-fried together.
He suddenly covered my hand on the spatula —
"The heat is just right — just like us."
That night's pasta was the best I'd ever tasted in my life.
At the school festival banquet, Leo was the student representative chef.
Before a hundred guests, he prepared a French dessert — and for the final touch, he carved my name into the sugar art.
"This dessert is called Heartbeat, dedicated to the one who makes my heart race."
He spoke into the microphone, his gaze cutting through the crowd straight to me.
The room erupted, and my face burned hotter than his stove.
After graduation, Leo took me to a newly opened restaurant — the sign above the door bore my name.
"I opened this for you."
From his pocket, he produced a key and a ring —
"The key is for the restaurant — and for my heart. The ring... if you'll have it."
Amid a shower of flower petals, he dropped to one knee —
"For every meal for the rest of my life, I want to taste it with you."
